papers.title: Weighted sum of independent indicator random variables
papers.why_relevant: Gives the directly portable three-colour coupling proof for the p=1/3 endpoint: couple three Bernoulli(1/3) vectors whose coordinatewise sum is always 1, then use symmetry.

papers.title: Small Deviations of Sums of Independent Random Variables
papers.why_relevant: Provides explicit polynomial majorants and moment computations for centered independent sums; the Bernoulli summands here have exactly tractable moments and positive third moment for p <= 1/3.

lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2 and equation (11)
lemmas.statement: Let ‚Ąď,r>0 and define the quartic polynomial Q_{‚Ąď,r}(x)=‚ąĎ_{i=0}^4 q_i x^i by q0=1, q1=2r¬≤(2‚Ąď+r)/(‚Ąď(‚Ąď+r)¬≥), q2=r(‚ąí8‚Ąď¬≤‚ąí‚Ąďr+r¬≤)/(‚Ąď¬≤(‚Ąď+r)¬≥), q3=(4‚Ąď¬≤‚ąí4‚Ąďr‚ąí2r¬≤)/(‚Ąď¬≤(‚Ąď+r)¬≥), and q4=(3‚Ąď+r)/(‚Ąď¬≤(‚Ąď+r)¬≥). Then Q_{‚Ąď,r}(x)‚Č•1_{x‚Č•0} for every real x; equivalently, Q_{‚Ąď,r}(x)‚Č•0 for all x and Q_{‚Ąď,r}(x)‚Č•1 for all x‚Č•0. Consequently Q_{‚Ąď,r}(x‚ąíőī)‚Č•1_{x‚Č•őī} for every real őī. In the special case ‚Ąď=r=s, Q_s(x)=1+3x/(4s)‚ąíx¬≤/s¬≤‚ąíx¬≥/(4s¬≥)+x‚Āī/(2s‚Āī). Also q4>0 always, and q3‚Č§0 whenever ‚Ąď‚Č§(1+‚ąö3)r/2, with strict inequality unless equality holds. Moreover, if X is any real random variable with E X=0 and finite fourth moment, then for every real őī, E[Q_{‚Ąď,r}(X‚ąíőī)]=‚ąĎ_{i=0}^4 (‚ąíőī)^i q_i +(q2‚ąí3őīq3+6őī¬≤q4)E[X¬≤]+(q3‚ąí4őīq4)E[X¬≥]+q4 E[X‚Āī].
lemmas.proof_sketch: The coefficients are chosen so that Q has a double root at ‚ąí‚Ąď, Q(0)=1, and Q‚ąí1 has a double root at r. Expanding these interpolation/tangency conditions gives the displayed q_i. The key proof is by factorization: Q_{‚Ąď,r}(x)=[(‚Ąď+x)¬≤((‚Ąď+r)¬≥‚ąí2(‚Ąď¬≤+3‚Ąďr+r¬≤)x+(3‚Ąď+r)x¬≤)]/[‚Ąď¬≤(‚Ąď+r)¬≥]. The denominator and square are nonnegative; the remaining quadratic has minimum at x=(‚Ąď¬≤+3‚Ąďr+r¬≤)/(3‚Ąď+r), where its value is [(3‚Ąď+r)(‚Ąď+r)¬≥‚ąí(‚Ąď¬≤+3‚Ąďr+r¬≤)¬≤]/(3‚Ąď+r)=(2‚Ąď‚Āī+4‚Ąď¬≥r+‚Ąď¬≤r¬≤)/(3‚Ąď+r)‚Č•0. Hence Q‚Č•0 on R. Similarly, Q_{‚Ąď,r}(x)‚ąí1=[x(4‚Ąď¬≤+2‚Ąďr+(3‚Ąď+r)x)(x‚ąír)¬≤]/[‚Ąď¬≤(‚Ąď+r)¬≥], which is ‚Č•0 for x‚Č•0, proving Q‚Č•1 on the half-line. The shifted majorization is immediate by replacing x by x‚ąíőī. The expectation identity follows by expanding E[(X‚ąíőī)^i] for i‚Č§4 and using E X=0; the E[X¬≤], E[X¬≥], and E[X‚Āī] coefficients collect to q2‚ąí3őīq3+6őī¬≤q4, q3‚ąí4őīq4, and q4 respectively. This is the paper's main reusable dual-polynomial device: tail probabilities are bounded by E Q, which is then reduced to moment estimates.

lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1
lemmas.statement: Let X be a non-degenerate real-valued random variable with E X=0, E[X¬≤]=ŌÉ¬≤>0, and E[X¬≥]‚Č•0. If E[X‚Āī]‚Č§cŌÉ‚Āī for some c>0, then Pr[X‚Č•0]‚Č§1‚ąí1/(2c). The bound is sharp: for any a>0 and any p0<1/2, the distribution taking values ‚ąía,0,a with probabilities p0,1‚ąí2p0,p0 has E[X¬≥]=0, E[X‚Āī]=cŌÉ‚Āī with c=1/(2p0), and Pr[X‚Č•0]=1‚ąí1/(2c).
lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Lemma 2.2 with the symmetric choice ‚Ąď=r=‚ąöc ŌÉ. Then Q(x)=1+3x/(4‚ąöc ŌÉ)‚ąíx¬≤/(cŌÉ¬≤)‚ąíx¬≥/(4c^{3/2}ŌÉ¬≥)+x‚Āī/(2c¬≤ŌÉ‚Āī) majorizes 1_{x‚Č•0}. Therefore Pr[X‚Č•0]‚Č§E Q(X). Since E X=0, the linear term vanishes. The quadratic term contributes ‚ąíŌÉ¬≤/(cŌÉ¬≤)=‚ąí1/c. The cubic coefficient is negative, and the assumption E[X¬≥]‚Č•0 makes the cubic contribution nonpositive, so it can be discarded in an upper bound. The fourth-moment hypothesis gives x‚Āī contribution at most (1/(2c¬≤ŌÉ‚Āī))¬∑cŌÉ‚Āī=1/(2c). Thus E Q(X)‚Č§1‚ąí1/c+1/(2c)=1‚ąí1/(2c). For sharpness, compute the moments of the three-point symmetric distribution: ŌÉ¬≤=2p0 a¬≤, E[X¬≥]=0, E[X‚Āī]=2p0 a‚Āī, hence c=E[X‚Āī]/ŌÉ‚Āī=1/(2p0), while Pr[X‚Č•0]=1‚ąíp0=1‚ąí1/(2c).

lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
lemmas.statement: Let X1,‚Ä¶,Xn be a 4-wise independent collection of real random variables, meaning every subcollection of at most four variables is mutually independent. Assume E[Xi]=0 and |Xi|‚Č§1 almost surely for each i. Put X=‚ąĎ_{i=1}^n Xi. If E[X¬≥]‚Č•0, then Pr[X‚Č•4/25]‚Č§5/6.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Let ŌÉ¬≤=E[X¬≤]. If ŌÉ=0 the claim is trivial. By 4-wise independence and centering, E[X‚Āī]=‚ąĎE[Xi‚Āī]+6‚ąĎ_{i<j}E[Xi¬≤]E[Xj¬≤]=3(‚ąĎE[Xi¬≤])¬≤+‚ąĎ(E[Xi‚Āī]‚ąí3E[Xi¬≤]¬≤)‚Č§3ŌÉ‚Āī+ŌÉ¬≤, since |Xi|‚Č§1 gives E[Xi‚Āī]‚Č§E[Xi¬≤]. Use Lemma 2.2 on Q_{‚Ąď,r}(X‚ąíőī) with őī=4/25. For all parameter choices used, q4>0 and q3‚ąí4őīq4<0; hence the hypothesis E[X¬≥]‚Č•0 allows the cubic term in the shifted expectation expansion to be dropped. Thus E Q_{‚Ąď,r}(X‚ąíőī)‚Č§‚ąĎ(‚ąíőī)^i q_i +(q2‚ąí3őīq3+6őī¬≤q4)ŌÉ¬≤+q4(3ŌÉ‚Āī+ŌÉ¬≤). It remains to choose ‚Ąď,r as functions of ŌÉ so this is ‚Č§5/6. For ŌÉ>5/4 take ‚Ąď=r=‚ąö3 ŌÉ, giving E Q‚Č§5/6+[2őī‚Āī+‚ąö3őī¬≥ŌÉ+2ŌÉ¬≤‚ąí6‚ąö3őīŌÉ¬≥]/(36ŌÉ‚Āī), and with őī=4/25 the bracket is ‚Č§0 using ŌÉ^{-1}<4/5. For 0.68‚Č§ŌÉ‚Č§1.25 take ‚Ąď=2ŌÉ, r=5ŌÉ/2; the upper bound becomes 835/972‚ąí226őī ŌÉ^{-1}/729+(68‚ąí207őī¬≤)ŌÉ^{-2}/2916+11őī¬≥ŌÉ^{-3}/243+17őī‚ĀīŌÉ^{-4}/729. As a function of t=ŌÉ^{-1} this is convex, and direct endpoint checks at ŌÉ=0.68 and 1.25 put it below 5/6. For 0.5‚Č§ŌÉ‚Č§0.68 take ‚Ąď=15ŌÉ/7, r=3ŌÉ; the analogous bound is 256957/291600‚ąí10633őī ŌÉ^{-1}/32400+(26411‚ąí128625őī¬≤)ŌÉ^{-2}/1749600+7889őī¬≥ŌÉ^{-3}/194400+26411őī‚ĀīŌÉ^{-4}/1749600, again convex in ŌÉ^{-1} and below 5/6 at the endpoints. For 0‚Č§ŌÉ‚Č§0.5 take fixed ‚Ąď=1,r=2, obtaining E Q‚Č§62573/78125‚ąí59ŌÉ¬≤/3375+5ŌÉ‚Āī/9‚Č§5/6. Since Pr[X‚Č•őī]‚Č§E Q_{‚Ąď,r}(X‚ąíőī), these cases prove the lemma.

papers.title: On Some Conjectures of Samuels and Feige
papers.why_relevant: Develops extremal reductions and product-type estimates for small-deviation problems, offering a template for reducing arbitrary weights to one-heavy-coordinate or small equal-weight obstructions.

lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
lemmas.statement: Let n‚Č•1, let őľ1‚Č•...‚Č•őľn>0 with ‚ąĎ_{i=1}^n őľ_i=1, and let X1,...,Xn be independent non-negative random variables with E X_i=1 for every i. Put Z=‚ąĎ_{i=1}^n őľ_i X_i, M=őľ1, let őī>0, and set T=1+őī. Assume that the following Samuels lower bound is valid for all such data: P(Z<T) ‚Č• min_{1‚Č§i‚Č§n} ő†_{j=1}^i (1 ‚ąí őľ_j/(T ‚ąí ‚ąĎ_{k=i+1}^n őľ_k)), where the tail sum is interpreted as 0 when i=n. Then the following Feige lower bound is valid for all such data: P(Z<T) ‚Č• min(őī/(őī+M), 1/e).
lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is local: fix one admissible choice and assume its Samuels product bound. Write ŌÉ_i=‚ąĎ_{k=1}^i őľ_k, so T‚ąí‚ąĎ_{k=i+1}^n őľ_k=ŌÉ_i+őī. Choose i attaining the minimum in Samuels' bound. Introduce ő¶(a,ŌĀ)=a^{-1} log(1‚ąía/(1+ŌĀ)) for a>0 and ő¶(0,ŌĀ)=‚ąí1/(1+ŌĀ). For j‚Č§i, with a_j=őľ_j/ŌÉ_i and ŌĀ=őī/ŌÉ_i, one has log(1‚ąíőľ_j/(ŌÉ_i+őī))=a_j ő¶(a_j,ŌĀ). Hence log P(Z<T) is at least ‚ąĎ_{j‚Č§i} a_j ő¶(a_j,ŌĀ). The key monotonicity input is that ő¶ is strictly decreasing in its first variable; this follows by writing ő¶(a,ŌĀ)=(1/(1+ŌĀ))ŌÜ(a/(1+ŌĀ)), where ŌÜ(s)=log(1‚ąís)/s=‚ąí‚ąĎ_{k‚Č•0}s^k/(k+1), a strictly decreasing power series on [0,1). Since a_j‚Č§M/ŌÉ_i and ‚ąĎ a_j=1, the weighted average is at least ő¶(M/ŌÉ_i,őī/ŌÉ_i). Now ŌÉ_i‚ąą[M,1]. Put t=M/ŌÉ_i and őĪ=őī/M; then the lower bound is at least min_{t‚ąą[M,1]} ő¶(t,őĪt). Lemma 3.4 says t‚Ü¶ő¶(t,őĪt) is concave, so its minimum over the interval is attained at an endpoint: min(ő¶(1,őī/M), ő¶(M,őī)). The first endpoint equals log(őī/(őī+M)); exponentiating gives the intermediate product lower bound min(őī/(őī+M), (1‚ąíM/(1+őī))^{1/M}). Finally Lemma 3.3 gives exp(ő¶(M,őī))‚Č•min(őī/(őī+M),1/e), yielding P(Z<T)‚Č•min(őī/(őī+M),1/e). Thus the argument actually proves the deterministic product estimate min_i ő†_{j‚Č§i}(1‚ąíőľ_j/(ŌÉ_i+őī))‚Č•min(őī/(őī+M),1/e), and Samuels' bound transfers it to the probability.

lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
lemmas.statement: Define ő¶:[0,1]√ó(0,‚ąě)‚ÜíR by ő¶(őľ,ŌĀ)=őľ^{-1} log(1‚ąíőľ/(1+ŌĀ)) for őľ‚ąą(0,1], and ő¶(0,ŌĀ)=‚ąí1/(1+ŌĀ), with log denoting the natural logarithm. Then for every őľ‚ąą[0,1] and every ŌĀ>0, ő¶(őľ,ŌĀ) ‚Č• log(min(ŌĀ/(őľ+ŌĀ), 1/e)).
lemmas.proof_sketch: The boundary cases are immediate: for őľ=0, ő¶=‚ąí1/(1+ŌĀ)>‚ąí1=log(1/e); for őľ=1, ő¶=log(ŌĀ/(1+ŌĀ)), which is at least the logarithm of the displayed minimum. Fix 0<őľ<1. First note that ‚ąāő¶/‚ąāŌĀ = 1/((1+ŌĀ)(1+ŌĀ‚ąíőľ))>0, so ő¶ increases with ŌĀ. Split at ŌĀ0=őľ/(e‚ąí1), the point where ŌĀ/(őľ+ŌĀ)=1/e. If ŌĀ‚Č•ŌĀ0, the desired lower bound is ő¶‚Č•‚ąí1. By monotonicity in ŌĀ it suffices to prove ő¶(őľ,ŌĀ0)‚Č•‚ąí1, i.e. 1‚ąíőľ/(1+őľ/(e‚ąí1))‚Č•e^{‚ąíőľ}. This is Lemma 3.1: for 0‚Č§t‚Č§1, 1‚ąít/(1+t/(e‚ąí1))‚Č•e^{‚ąít}. The paper proves it by setting f(t)=(1+t/(e‚ąí1))(1‚ąíe^{‚ąít})‚ąít, computing f(0)=f(1)=0 and f''(t)=(e^{‚ąít}/(e‚ąí1))(3‚ąíe‚ąít), so f is first convex then concave and stays nonnegative. If 0<ŌĀ‚Č§ŌĀ0, the desired lower bound is ő¶(őľ,ŌĀ)‚Č•log(ŌĀ/(őľ+ŌĀ)). Define g(ŌĀ)=ő¶(őľ,ŌĀ)‚ąílog(ŌĀ/(őľ+ŌĀ)). A direct derivative computation gives g'(ŌĀ)=‚ąí(1‚ąíőľ)((1+ŌĀ)őľ‚ąíŌĀ^2)/(ŌĀ(1+ŌĀ)(1+ŌĀ‚ąíőľ)(őľ+ŌĀ)). Since ŌĀ‚Č§őľ/(e‚ąí1)<őľ, the numerator factor ((1+ŌĀ)őľ‚ąíŌĀ^2) is positive, so g'<0 on (0,ŌĀ0]. Hence g(ŌĀ)‚Č•g(ŌĀ0). But g(ŌĀ0)=ő¶(őľ,ŌĀ0)+1‚Č•0 by the previous case, completing the proof.

lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
lemmas.statement: For every őĪ>0, define h_őĪ:[0,1]‚ÜíR by h_őĪ(0)=‚ąí1 and h_őĪ(t)=t^{-1} log(1‚ąít/(1+őĪt)) for 0<t‚Č§1; equivalently, h_őĪ(t)=ő¶(t,őĪt) for t>0, with the value at 0 understood by continuous extension. Then h_őĪ is concave on [0,1].
lemmas.proof_sketch: The extension at 0 is continuous because log(1‚ąít/(1+őĪt))=‚ąít+O(t^2). The function is smooth on (0,1), so it is enough to prove h_őĪ''(t)‚Č§0 there. Rather than inspect h_őĪ'' directly, the proof multiplies by t^3 and sets ő∑(t)=t^3 h_őĪ''(t). Direct differentiation gives ő∑(t)= t(2+(6őĪ‚ąí3)t+4(őĪ^2‚ąíőĪ)t^2)/((1‚ąít+őĪt)^2(1+őĪt)^2) + 2 log(1‚ąít/(1+őĪt)). Differentiating this expression produces the decisive factorization ő∑'(t)=‚ąít^2(1+3(1‚ąí2őĪ+2(őĪ‚ąíőĪ^2)t)^2)/(2(1‚ąít+őĪt)^3(1+őĪt)^3). For őĪ>0 and 0<t<1 both denominator factors are positive, and the numerator is strictly positive, so ő∑'(t)<0. Since lim_{s‚Üď0} ő∑(s)=0, it follows that ő∑(t)<0 for every t‚ąą(0,1), hence h_őĪ''(t)<0 on (0,1). Continuity at the endpoints then gives concavity on the whole interval. A frequently used corollary is that for any subinterval [a,b]‚äÜ[0,1], min_{t‚ąą[a,b]} h_őĪ(t)=min(h_őĪ(a),h_őĪ(b)).

papers.title: On Sums of Independent Random Variables with Unbounded Variance, and Estimating the Average Degree in a Graph
papers.why_relevant: Feige's lower-tail machinery, especially support reduction, modified merging, and one-zero product bounds for two-point variables, is closely aligned with the complemented exact-mean formulation of the problem.

lemmas.label: Lemma 4 (reduce support)
lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be independent nonnegative random variables with finite supports, let X=ő£_j X_j and őľ=E[X], and fix őī>0. Suppose that for some index i, X_i has support {v_1,...,v_k} with k‚Č•3. Then X_i can be replaced by a random variable X_i' that is independent of {X_j:j‚Č†i}, has E[X_i']=E[X_i], and has support consisting of at most two values from {v_1,...,v_k}, in such a way that, writing X'=X_i'+ő£_{j‚Č†i} X_j, one has E[X']=őľ and Pr[X'<őľ+őī]‚Č§Pr[X<őľ+őī].
lemmas.proof_sketch: For each support point v_j of X_i, let q_j be the conditional probability of the target lower-tail event when X_i is fixed to v_j: q_j=Pr[ő£_{‚Ąď‚Č†i} X_‚Ąď+v_j<őľ+őī]. A replacement distribution on the same support is specified by probabilities p_j. Preserving total mass and mean imposes the two linear constraints ő£_j p_j=1 and ő£_j p_j v_j=E[X_i], with p_j‚Č•0. The resulting lower-tail probability is the linear objective ő£_j q_j p_j. The original distribution of X_i is feasible, so the minimum of this linear program is at most the original lower-tail probability. A basic optimal solution of an LP with two equality constraints has at most two positive p_j's (apart from degeneracy, still no more than two are needed). Taking X_i' with this basic optimal distribution gives the claim. The same LP idea is later reused in the paper with the objective Pr[sum<mean] instead of Pr[sum<mean+őī], yielding the modified reduce-support operation.

lemmas.label: Proposition 8
lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be independent random variables. For each i, let őľ_i=E[X_i], assume őľ_1‚Č•őľ_2‚Č•...‚Č•őľ_n, and assume X_i takes value őľ_i+s_i with probability őľ_i/(őľ_i+s_i) and value 0 otherwise, where s_i‚Č•0, with the evident degenerate interpretation when őľ_i=s_i=0. Let X=ő£_i X_i, őľ=ő£_i őľ_i, and s=ő£_i s_i. Then Pr[X<őľ‚ąíőľ_n+s]‚Č•s/(őľ_1+s).
lemmas.proof_sketch: The maximum possible value of X is őľ+s=ő£_i(őľ_i+s_i). If some variable with s_i>0 comes up 0, the sum loses őľ_i+s_i, which is strictly larger than őľ_n because őľ_i‚Č•őľ_n and s_i>0; hence X<őľ+s‚ąíőľ_n. Variables with s_i=0 are at their upper value almost surely. Therefore the event X‚Č•őľ+s‚ąíőľ_n can occur only when every variable with positive surplus takes its upper value, so Pr[X‚Č•őľ+s‚ąíőľ_n]‚Č§ő†_i őľ_i/(őľ_i+s_i). Since őľ_i‚Č§őľ_1 and a/(a+t) is increasing in a for t‚Č•0, each factor is at most őľ_1/(őľ_1+s_i). For fixed total surplus s, ő†_i őľ_1/(őľ_1+s_i)‚Č§őľ_1/(őľ_1+s); equivalently, combining two surpluses x,y cannot decrease the product because (őľ_1/(őľ_1+x))(őľ_1/(őľ_1+y))‚Č§őľ_1/(őľ_1+x+y). Thus Pr[X‚Č•őľ+s‚ąíőľ_n]‚Č§őľ_1/(őľ_1+s), and taking complements gives Pr[X<őľ‚ąíőľ_n+s]‚Č•1‚ąíőľ_1/(őľ_1+s)=s/(őľ_1+s).

lemmas.label: Lemma 10 (stage-2 modified merge bound)
lemmas.statement: Consider the following Stage 2 process on a finite collection of independent nonconstant two-point random variables. At any time, variable X_i has mean őľ_i>0 and takes values 0 and őľ_i+s_i, where s_i‚Č•0 is its surplus. Let X=ő£_i X_i, őľ=ő£_i őľ_i, and let őľ_1=max_i őľ_i. Initially all means lie in [őľ_1/2, őľ_1], and the total surplus is at least the initial minimum mean; in particular, for őĪ=1/3, the initial value of s'=ő£_{i: őľ_i‚Č•őľ_1/2} s_i is greater than őĪ őľ_1. A modified merge step chooses the two variables of currently smallest means, replaces them by a variable distributed as their sum, applies the support-reduction LP with objective Pr[new total < new mean] to reduce this replacement to at most two support points without increasing that exact lower-tail probability, and then aligns the resulting two-point variable with 0 by subtracting its lower support point from that variable and from the total mean, or removes it if it is constant. After each step redefine őľ_1=max_i őľ_i and s'=ő£_{i: őľ_i‚Č•őľ_1/2} s_i. Run modified merge steps until either only one nonconstant variable remains or s'‚Č§őĪ őľ_1. For őĪ=1/3, if r is the stopping time, then either Pr[X^r<őľ^r]‚Č•1/13, or, if the stopping time was reached by a last modified merge, the collection one step before stopping satisfies Pr[X^{r‚ąí1}<őľ^{r‚ąí1}]‚Č•1/13.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Each modified merge preserves or decreases the exact lower-tail probability Pr[X<őľ]: the LP is chosen to minimize that probability at the same mean, and aligning with 0 or removing a constant shifts both X and őľ by the same deterministic amount. The process maintains that at most one variable has mean below őľ_1/2: when two smallest means are merged, all unmerged variables have mean at least half of any new maximum. Set ő≤=1/8. If the process stops with one variable while s'>őĪőľ_1, then Pr[X<őľ]=Pr[this variable is 0]=s'/(őľ_1+s')>őĪ/(1+őĪ). If it stops with ő≤őľ_1‚Č§s'‚Č§őĪőľ_1, write s'=ő≥őľ_1. Apply Proposition 8 to the variables with mean at least őľ_1/2: with probability at least ő≥/(1+ő≥), their partial sum is below its mean by (1/2‚ąíő≥)őľ_1. The only possible spoiler is the single low-mean variable. If its mean is at most s', Markov bounds the probability that it fills this gap by at most 2ő≥, giving Pr[X<őľ]‚Č•(ő≥‚ąí2ő≥^2)/(1+ő≥); if its mean is larger than s', its zero atom gives an even larger bound. Over ő≥‚ąą[ő≤,őĪ], this is minimized at an endpoint and is at least 1/12 for őĪ=1/3, ő≤=1/8. It remains to handle stopping with s'<ő≤őľ_1. Look one step earlier, normalize the old maximum mean to 1, and let the merged variables have means őľ_i‚Č•őľ_j. Before the last merge, s'‚Č•őĪ; surplus outside the merged pair is at most ő≤ times the new maximum. If őľ_j<1/2, then only X_i among the pair counted toward s' before the merge, so s_i‚Č•őĪ‚ąíő≤(new maximum). The event X_i=0, together with X_j failing to compensate the deficit after allowing the outside surplus, forces X<őľ; optimizing over the endpoint possibilities őľ_i=1/2 or 1 yields the bounds ((őĪ‚ąíő≤)/(1/2+őĪ‚ąíő≤))*((1/2‚ąíő≤)/(1‚ąíő≤)) and ((őĪ‚ąí3ő≤/2)/(1+őĪ‚ąí3ő≤/2))*((1‚ąí3ő≤/2)/(3/2‚ąí3ő≤/2)). If őľ_j‚Č•1/2, both merged variables counted, and s_i+s_j‚Č•őĪ‚ąíő≤(őľ_i+őľ_j)‚Č•őĪ‚ąí2ő≤. If their pair sum is below (1‚ąíő≤)(őľ_i+őľ_j), the outside variables cannot restore the total to őľ. Depending on whether a zero of either variable suffices, exactly one zero suffices, or both zeros are needed, one obtains the bounds (őĪ‚ąí2ő≤)/(1+őĪ‚ąí2ő≤), ((1/2‚ąíő≤)/(1‚ąíő≤))^2, and ((1/2‚ąí3ő≤/2)/(3/2‚ąí3ő≤/2))*((1‚ąí3ő≤/2)/(3/2‚ąí3ő≤/2)); the one-zero-only subcases are dominated. Substituting őĪ=1/3 and ő≤=1/8 makes every displayed bound at least 1/13, with (őĪ‚ąí2ő≤)/(1+őĪ‚ąí2ő≤)=1/13.

papers.title: An improved bound for the Manickam-Mikl√≥s-Singhi conjecture
papers.why_relevant: Contains the complement-pair argument proving the p=1/2 endpoint, plus Baranyai and fixed-size-layer techniques that can be applied after conditioning on the number of selected Bernoulli variables.

lemmas.label: Complement-pair observation (Introduction)
lemmas.statement: Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with total sum ‚ąĎ_{i=1}^n x_i ‚Č• 0. Then at least 2^{n-1} subsets S ‚äÜ [n] satisfy ‚ąĎ_{i in S} x_i ‚Č• 0.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Partition the power set of [n] into the 2^{n-1} unordered complementary pairs {S,[n]\S}. For each pair, the two subset sums add to the total sum ‚ąĎ_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Hence the two sums cannot both be negative, so every complementary pair contributes at least one subset with nonnegative sum. This gives at least one nonnegative subset from each of 2^{n-1} pairs. Under the usual convention that the empty sum is 0, the pair {‚ąÖ,[n]} may contribute two subsets, but the lower bound needed in the paper is 2^{n-1}. For the weighted Bernoulli p=1/2 endpoint, the same pairing says that for every subset S, the weights of S and its complement add to 1, so at least one has weight at least 1/2.

lemmas.label: Baranyai partition corollary / multiple-of-k case
lemmas.statement: Let n and k be positive integers with k dividing n. Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with ‚ąĎ_{i=1}^n x_i ‚Č• 0. Then at least C(n-1,k-1) of the k-element subsets S ‚äÜ [n] satisfy ‚ąĎ_{i in S} x_i ‚Č• 0. Equivalently, in the notation A(n,k) for the minimum possible number of nonnegative k-sums over all n-tuples of real numbers with nonnegative total sum, one has A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) whenever k divides n.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Baranyai's partition theorem: when k|n, the family [n]^{(k)} of all k-subsets can be partitioned into parallel classes, each class consisting of n/k pairwise disjoint k-sets whose union is [n]. The number of parallel classes is C(n,k)/(n/k) = (k/n)C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1). In any one parallel class, the sums of the n/k disjoint k-sets add exactly to the total sum ‚ąĎ_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Therefore not all k-set sums in that class can be negative; each class contains at least one nonnegative k-sum. Since the classes partition [n]^{(k)}, the nonnegative k-sets found in distinct classes are distinct, giving at least C(n-1,k-1) of them. The matching upper-bound example is x_1=n-1 and x_2=...=x_n=-1: for n‚Č•k, exactly the k-subsets containing x_1 have nonnegative sum, and there are C(n-1,k-1) such subsets.

lemmas.label: Theorem 3 (main bound for the Manickam-Mikl√≥s-Singhi conjecture; typo-corrected statement)
lemmas.statement: Let k‚Č•2 and n be positive integers, and let log denote the natural logarithm. If n > k(4e log k)^k, then every collection x_1,...,x_n of n real numbers with ‚ąĎ_{i=1}^n x_i ‚Č• 0 has at least C(n-1,k-1) k-element subsets S ‚äÜ [n] with ‚ąĎ_{i in S} x_i ‚Č• 0. Equivalently, A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) for all n > k(4e log k)^k. The paper's displayed theorem has an evident typo in the first quantifier; this is the form stated in the abstract and proved by the argument.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Order x_1‚Č•...‚Č•x_n. For any ordered finite set Y={y_1‚Č•...‚Č•y_N} with nonnegative total, the basic inequality y_1 ‚Č• -((N-j)/j)y_{j+1} follows from j y_1+(N-j)y_{j+1} ‚Č• ‚ąĎY ‚Č•0. Call an element central in Y if every k-subset of Y containing it has nonnegative sum. If x_1 is central in X, the C(n-1,k-1) k-sets containing x_1 finish the proof. Otherwise, since x_1 is the largest element, x_1 plus the k‚ąí1 smallest elements has negative sum; delete these k elements. The remaining set has nonnegative total. Iterate: X_1=X; if the largest remaining element x_i is not central in X_i, delete it and the k‚ąí1 smallest elements of X_i to form X_{i+1}. If at stage p+1 the largest element x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, whose size is n‚ąíkp, then for every (k‚ąí1)-subset T of X_{p+1}\{x_{p+1}} and every r‚Č§p+1, the set T‚ą™{x_r} is nonnegative, because x_r‚Č•x_{p+1}. These families are disjoint, so there are at least (p+1)C(n‚ąíkp‚ąí1,k‚ąí1) nonnegative k-sets. For 1‚Č§p‚Č§n/(2k), the paper proves this is already larger than C(n‚ąí1,k‚ąí1): using C(n‚ąíkp‚ąí1,k‚ąí1) ‚Č• (n‚ąík(p+1))^{k‚ąí1}/(k‚ąí1)! and C(n‚ąí1,k‚ąí1) ‚Č§ n^{k‚ąí1}/(k‚ąí1)!, it suffices to show (p+1)(n‚ąík(p+1))^{k‚ąí1}>n^{k‚ąí1}. If p<n/k^2, lower-bound (n‚ąía)^{k‚ąí1} by its first term minus second term, with a=k(p+1), valid here once n>k^3; the condition reduces to n/[k(k‚ąí1)]>p+2+1/p, implied by p<n/k^2 and n>3k^2(k‚ąí1). If n/k^2‚Č§p<n/(2k), use p+1‚Č•n/k^2 and, with harmless integer endpoint rounding, n‚ąík(p+1)‚Č•n/2 to get a lower bound n^k/(2^{k‚ąí1}k^2)>n^{k‚ąí1} when n>2^{k‚ąí1}k^2. Hence, unless the theorem is already proved, no central element appears in the first about n/(2k) iterations. In that case, after q=n/(2k) deletions the current set has nonnegative total and size about n/2. Apply the basic inequality to it with j=n/(2 log k) to get x_q ‚Č• ‚ąí(log k‚ąí1)x_{q+j}. Thus any k-set formed by choosing a=k/log k elements from the first block {x_1,...,x_q} and b=k‚ąía elements from the next block of length j has nonnegative sum, since b=a(log k‚ąí1) and each chosen first-block element is at least x_q while each chosen second-block element is at least x_{q+j}. The number of such k-sets is C(n/(2k), k/log k) C(n/(2 log k), k‚ąík/log k). The paper lower-bounds this by n^k/k! ¬∑ (1/(4k))^{k/log k}(1/(4 log k))^{k‚ąík/log k} > n^k/[k!(4e log k)^k], using k^{k/log k}=e^k and routine binomial estimates. If n>k(4e log k)^k, this exceeds n^{k‚ąí1}/(k‚ąí1)! ‚Č• C(n‚ąí1,k‚ąí1), completing the proof. Floor/ceiling issues in quantities such as n/(2k) and k/log k are suppressed in the paper and are handled by routine rounding.
